title: Twin Pioneer Bed
summary: A simple twin bed with a gently curved headboard top and a matching flat-topped footboard, built from 1x stock with no plywood.
description:
This bed keeps the joinery honest and simple — legs, a curved or flat panel, and top trim, repeated for both the headboard and footboard — which makes it a good first bed-frame build. The headboard's curve is the one place that takes real care: mark it out generously, cut with a jigsaw staying outside the line, then sand progressively from coarse to fine grit until the curve reads as smooth and intentional rather than jigsawed.

The side rails support the mattress two ways depending on what you're using: position the cleats at the base of the rail if you're running a box spring, or 1-1/2" from the top if you're going bare-slat with no box spring. Get that cleat height decision made before you cut, since it changes where the rails attach relative to the headboard and footboard.

cutList.note: One cut with a gentle curved top.

cutList.part: Slats (optional, if not using a box spring)

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body:
Cut two headboard rails 40-1/2" from the 1x8 — both come off the one 8 ft board — the footboard panel 40-1/2" from the 4 ft 1x10, and two side rails 75" from the 1x6, one per 8 ft board. From 1x3 cut two headboard front legs 33-1/2" and two footboard front legs 16-3/4". From 1x2 cut two headboard side legs 33-1/2", two footboard side legs 16-3/4", eight top trim pieces 2-1/4" and two cleats 75"; that is everything three 8 ft 1x2s will yield, with roughly an inch to spare, so cut the two 75" cleats first. Leave the curve alone for now — cut the headboard rail square to 40-1/2" and shape it in the next step.

The fifteen 39" slats are not covered by the listed stock. Two 39" slats fit an 8 ft 1x3, so the slats alone want eight boards on top of the two the legs consume, against the two 1x3s in the materials list — buy about seven more if you are building without a box spring.

steps.title: Build the headboard
steps.body: Mark a gentle curved top on one of the 1x8 headboard rails and cut it out carefully with a jigsaw. Sand the curved edge smooth, working from coarse to fine sandpaper. Lay the two headboard rails (one curved, one straight) across the 1x3 front legs, leaving 3/4" clear on the outside of each leg — using the 1x2 side-leg board as a spacing guide helps here. Nail on with 1-1/4" nails and glue, or 1-1/4" screws and glue.

steps.title: Attach the headboard side legs
steps.body: The 1x2 side legs are the same 33-1/2" as the front legs, and each lands on the outside edge of a front leg — filling the 3/4" you held the rails back by and covering the rail ends. Glue and drive 2" finish nails through the 1x2 into the edge of the 1x3, keeping both tops flush. Built this way each post reads 2-1/2" across the front and 2-1/4" deep on the side, and no end grain shows on the outside of the bed.

steps.title: Add the headboard top trim
steps.body: There are eight 2-1/4" trim pieces and four posts, so each post top takes two — one on the front face and one on the side, meeting at the corner. Nail each piece on separately with 2" finish nails and glue rather than building a cap and dropping it over; the four posts will not be identical to the thousandth, and fitting piece by piece hides that. Keep every trim piece flush with the post top or very slightly proud, never below it.

steps.title: Build the footboard
steps.body: Build the footboard exactly like the headboard, using the single 40-1/2" 1x10 panel in place of the two 1x8 rails and the shorter 16-3/4" legs. Hold the panel back 3/4" from the outside edge of each 1x3 front leg, same as the headboard, so the 1x2 side legs land in the same relationship. There is no curve at this end — the top edge stays straight, which is the only difference between the two ends of the bed.

steps.title: Attach the side rails and cleats
steps.body: Screw a 75" 1x2 cleat along the inside face of each 75" side rail with 1-1/4" screws and glue, a screw every 10" or so. Put the cleat at the bottom edge of the rail if a box spring is going in, or 1-1/2" down from the top edge if you are running slats — decide before you drill, because it sets the finished mattress height. Attach the rails to the inside faces of the headboard and footboard legs so the clear opening between the two rails comes out at 39", which is exactly the slat length; check it with a tape before the screws go home. Metal bed-rail brackets in place of screws let the whole bed knock down for moving.

steps.title: Add the slats
steps.body: If you are skipping the box spring, lay the fifteen 39" slats across the cleats with one at each end and the rest spread evenly along the 75" rails. Fifteen 2-1/2" slats occupy 37-1/2", which leaves 37-1/2" of gap across fourteen spaces — about 2-11/16" each, a shade wider than the source's rough 2". Screw each slat down with one 1-1/4" screw per end and no glue, so a cracked slat can be swapped out later.

images.alt: A simple twin bed with a gently curved headboard and matching footboard
